title: Club sandwich
subtitle: Three-decker chicken, bacon, lettuce, tomato, and mayonnaise on toasted white
excerpt: The three-tier American club: toasted white bread layered with roast chicken, crisp streaky bacon, lettuce, tomato, and a heavy spread of mayonnaise. A second piece of toast in the middle is the structural element that holds it together. Skewered with a cocktail stick to stay upright.
sourceNotes: The club sandwich appears in American hotel and club dining from the 1890s. An early printed version appears in 'Good Housekeeping' magazine (1903) and subsequent American cookery literature throughout the early twentieth century. It became a fixture of diner and hotel-room service menus by the 1950s. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) documents the American sandwich tradition that produced it.

content.text: The defining feature of a club sandwich is the middle slice of toast. Without it, you have a very tall BLT with chicken. With it, you have a properly structured three-tier sandwich where each layer is self-contained: the bottom half carries chicken and mayonnaise, the top half carries bacon, lettuce, and tomato with more mayonnaise, and the middle slice of toast keeps the layers from pressing into each other when you cut it.

Use leftover roast chicken or poached chicken breast if you have it. If not, a 15-minute poach (a chicken breast in simmering salted water, covered, until it reaches 74°C inside) produces exactly the right texture: firm enough to slice thinly, tender enough not to tear the bread.

items.prepNote: cooked and thinly sliced (leftover roast chicken works well)
white sandwich bread, all slices toasted

content.text: Cook the bacon and toast the bread
Lay the streaky bacon in a cold frying pan over medium heat and cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side until properly crisp. Drain on kitchen paper. Toast all six slices of bread. Keep them warm.

Spread {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise on one side of all six toast slices. Season the tomato with salt and pepper. On two slices of toast (mayonnaise-side up), arrange the sliced chicken. Season with salt and pepper. Place a second slice of toast on top, mayonnaise-side up. Add the lettuce, tomato, and bacon. Top with the final slice of toast, mayonnaise-side down. Press gently and insert a cocktail stick through each quarter before cutting to hold the layers in place. Cut into four triangles.

items.cause: No cocktail sticks, or cut without the sticks in place
items.fix: Insert the sticks before cutting, one per quarter; they hold the three tiers together while you slice

items.symptom: Middle layer of toast is soggy
items.cause: Sandwich assembled too far in advance, or tomato not seasoned and drained
items.fix: Assemble just before serving; season the tomato slices and pat dry with kitchen paper to reduce moisture transfer

items.cause: Overcooked, or chicken breast used cold from the fridge without resting
items.fix: Poach chicken breast gently just to 74°C; allow it to cool in the stock before slicing — the residual moisture in the stock keeps it tender

items.symptom: Club feels stingy — too little filling
items.cause: Not enough chicken or bacon per sandwich
items.fix: A proper club sandwich is generous; use a full chicken breast and three rashers of bacon per serving

content.text: The club sandwich has been a hotel and club dining-room staple since the 1890s, when American establishments began serving sandwiches as a proper course rather than a working-class convenience. The three-layer format was practical: it gave the kitchen a vehicle for leftover roast chicken, and it gave the diner a substantial portion that could be eaten without a knife and fork. The four-triangle cut became the standard presentation because it fitted the service plates of American diners and gave each segment a visible cross-section of the fillings. It remains one of the most-ordered sandwiches in American hotel room service, virtually unchanged.
